item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ok but does anyone wanna take a sip of Compound Z21-Violet?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a great article, and the only issue that I have with it is that the secondary effects of SCP-2798 don't actually seem that bad, and the build-up to them seems unwarranted. I really admire the rabbit-hole that led me to this (1322, 2002, 2003, multiple tales, a 001 proposal, and now this). It really gives the feel of an interconnected universe. I also like the horror aspect of it, even though it was mostly only present in the text logs at the bottom. Incidentally, "held down and fucked by thousands of people at once" is never a thing I thought would creep me out so much (I never thought I'd read it either, but here I am).

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, what? Just another car on a hype train, complete with the kind of countdown you'd see on a website for a movie or game release. This would be much better if not for the fact that it just feels like an advertisement.

item.title: Re: in-universe: how exactly does this fit in?
item.content:encoded:
it's affixed to something canon

There is no canon, not even the mainlist.

If it helps, you can think of the mainlist as being pulled from numerous iterations of the SCPverse. The multiverse is a proven fact for the Foundation, and even within a single timeline, you have all sorts of anomalies constantly rewriting reality. (Note that in a number of scips, like many of the recent doomsday contest entries, it's implied that the article even originates from a terminated timeline.) Even from the narrowest viewpoint, it makes sense that they'd have at least a few entries dedicated to anomalies encountered in other timelines, on the off chance that the same thing happens in their own universe.

item.title: Re: in-universe: how exactly does this fit in?
item.content:encoded:
Not every work exists within the same world/universe. We have, for example, several SCPs set within conflicting and mutually exclusive canons. SCP-3241 is set within the Broken Masquerade Canon, and therefore explicitly conflicts with every single work that has a post-2014 timeframe, as well as any SCP set in the Third Law, Twisted Pines, S&C Plastics, Rat's Nest, The Gulf, AIAD, On Mount Golgotha, Project Heimdall or Dread and Circuses. (And every single one of those canons have SCPs)

In your example, 8346 and 2798 would then be mutually exclusive and wouldn't coexist. The O5 would either see something else in this slot or slot would be empty.

item.title: Re: in-universe: how exactly does this fit in?
item.content:encoded:
But that doesn't really seem…right. Like, it doesn't seem okay.

Something like SCP-001:O5 is fine (imo, obviously) when it affirms something is the true 001 because not only is it a 001 proposal itself, but it's a tale, it's not part of any mainlist (i.e. canon) piece. Say I wrote 001:O5 and I affixed it to my own skip, IDFK - SCP-3592 because why not. Now it's affixed to something canon, so if in-universe some O5 reads SCP-3592 and goes "Alright so the Factory is 001" and then reads 2798 and goes "Alright so alien overlords are 001", and then reads SCP-178 and goes "Okay so, like, another factory is 001" - how? What is the significance and reaction and etc. in-universe? How does that work?

item.title: Re: in-universe: how exactly does this fit in?
item.content:encoded: Each individual article exists in its own self-consistent setting, same as with any piece of fiction.

item.title: Re: in-universe: how exactly does this fit in?
item.content:encoded: I notice you say "this Foundation". Mind explaining that? Are you saying that each individual SCP is subject to existing in its author's own sort of Foundation head-canon or…what?

item.title: Re: in-universe: how exactly does this fit in?
item.content:encoded: Yes, so in this Foundation that's SCP-001.

item.title: Re: in-universe: how exactly does this fit in?
item.content:encoded: This one's a Thaumiel, which are commonly considered to be known to only high level personnel. In the mini-universe of 2798, Kal's 001 is the actual true 001, not a proposal (which is an out-of-universe term more often than not, I think). Anyone who's in the know about this one, in-universe, probably gets to cut past the "which 001 is the real deal" smokescreens and access "Kalinin's Proposal" as simply "SCP-001".

item.title: Re: in-universe: how exactly does this fit in?
item.content:encoded: True, but it links to a specific proposal. If it didn't link to Kalinin's Prop. we'd have no issue, it'd never say what 001 actually is and how it relates to it specifically (although I'd still have an issue to that claim it tried to 'negotiate' w/ 001 at some point)

item.title: Re: in-universe: how exactly does this fit in?
item.content:encoded:
In-document this just says "001".

Because it says on the 001 hub that the proposals are meant to be fake files serving as distractions from the real 001 — in which case we're right back to where we started.

This is putting way too much weight on the framing device for a hub.

item.title: in-universe: how exactly does this fit in?
item.content:encoded:
I love this scip, I +1'd it the day it was published, but now I'm curious…

Since this is a mainlist where SCP-001 is integral to the whole thing, what is this seen as in-universe? Since there's no SCP-001, what really is this? Is it a joke? A prank? While I understand canon/OOC isn't exactly 1:1, if this mainlist confirms a 001 as actually being 001, why does it link to a 001 proposal? Shouldn't it be 001 itself? When the in-univ. people of the Foundation are looking at this file, what are they supposed to take from it?

Or am I missing an in-universe component of what a 001 "proposal" actually is? OOC it's someone taking a crack at what they think something as mighty as SCP-001 would be. But, in-universe, are the proposals… literal proposals for what should be classified as 001? Like actual SCPs that they're just saying should be designated 001 instead of, say, 4739 or something? Because it says on the 001 hub that the proposals are meant to be fake files serving as distractions from the real 001 — in which case we're right back to where we started.

How can something just magically make a canon 001? How is this seen in character, in the Foundation universe?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually liked this one better than the proposal it was building up to.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm conflicted on this one. I like it as it is but I can't in good conscience upvote it because it is an SCP specifically created to hype up a specific 001 proposal (and it always was, even if the proposal wasn't complete yet, because the timer was there from day one), and having a main page SCP like that feels… wrong, you know? The whole point of the 001 project is that the 'real' 001 is supposed to be indeterminate and this, in a way, 'canonizes' it (yeah, yeah, nothing is canon).

doesn't help that the proposal in question is like a combination of Clef's proposal and the Thing What Makes SCPs proposals

-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sure, that too. If the Foundation creates fake 001 proposals, it stands to reason it can also create fake SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It could just be interpreted as part of the smokescreen, insert a skippy into the main list referencing one of the "fake" proposals, to distract from the "real" proposal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The fact that this SCP refers to a 001 proposal doesn't mean that it's promoting it as the 001 proposal (though I guess I understand why it might seem that way to some at first).

Like Crayne said up-thread:

The only difference is that now if you don't like Kalinin's proposal, you're not going to headcanon 2798 either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That it started unattached to any specific SCP-001 does make me think better of it, although I stand by initial point, I can see where others are coming from much more clearly. I'm going to remove my down vote, it's not a bad article, I, personally, just don't like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not only that, but the author was intentionally creating a big, suspenseful narrative. This is part of Kal's Proposal. This is part of a larger serial narrative. This is not a standalone piece. This isn't part of the vague mythos of the rest of the site, it's its own thing that just so happens to be happening in one version of the SCP universe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
attempt to promote someone's specific SCP-001

Well, Kalinin did make this and the proposal.

none of them are referenced by part of the main database

Technically, with the Wikiwalk program, SCP-718 now links directly to a 001 proposal.

which doesn't really make any of them canonical

There is no canon. You can believe anything that isn't explicitly set in stone, and even then you can make deviations.

putting this into the main database would imply that the SCP-001 it links to is the "real" one and it feels dirty to do so.

Even if it did link to it, the proposal still could be entirely bullshit.

just because it uses SCP-001

It actually didn't use a specific 001 for a while, and was pretty ambiguous until Kanlinin produced his proposal.

It would work a lot better if it was tied to an unidentified SCP, forcing us to imagine exactly what the foundation was trying to contain.

As I mentioned above, it didn't use a specific 001 for a while, so it was technically an "unidentified" SCP.

EDIT: Dammit MrWrong, stop ninjaing me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Technically, this came out way before the associated 001 proposal. When this article came out, it was genuinely tied to an unidentified SCP which happens to be called 'SCP-001'.

Besides, if 001 proposals can be crosslinked onto the rest of the mainlist, there's no rule that a mainlister cannot crosslink to an 001 proposal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This kinda feels like an attempt to promote someone's specific SCP-001 proposal, especially considering it links directly to said proposal. I have to say, it feels… like cheating? The other proposals stand on their own and at most reference other works, none of them are referenced by part of the main database, which doesn't really make any of them canonical. Putting this into the main database would imply that the SCP-001 it links to is the "real" one and it feels dirty to do so. Maybe that's just me, but I just… don't like it, just because it uses SCP-001. It would work a lot better if it was tied to an unidentified SCP, forcing us to imagine exactly what the foundation was trying to contain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Especially considering the fact that the moon's rapidly deteriorated, which would imply that it was somehow killed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can state that, having seen the counter post-finish, my initial reaction was, "oh shit." If that was the author's intention, it worked.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nope, but that criticism needs to be grounded in something. If that criticism is grounded in absolutely nothing, the criticism as a blanket observation becomes invalid. Not your vote, not your opinion, but the basis on which you appear to base those.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know how headcannons work, yes, however, that doesn't make it immune to criticism.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're not getting it, and I doubt we'll be able to explain it to you. Have fun.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But it also takes up space for other SCPs that don't rely on something intentionally left ambiguous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think I ever said this but my favorite part of this is how they dug up Django Reinhardt nonchalantly

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So? That's still the case? The only difference is that now if you don't like Kalinin's proposal, you're not going to headcanon 2798 either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't really like it; it assumes that there is an actual SCP-001. The fun of SCP-001 was that there was no real SCP-001; it was up for the reader to decide.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Guess I'm late to this party, considering all the new hubbub about Kal's 001 proposal is what brought me here.

That in mind, part of me was looking into this as a jumping off point for that serial, an end in which I think this article succeeds in its world building and in getting me as a reader pumped for its continuation.

On its own, I think this article is also is pretty good, creating a tense environment in which numerous horrible side effects to the field generated by 2798 is preferred to the effects of the ever mysterious SCP-001. Of particular enjoyment was the list of SCPs essentially used as ingredients in Project Serapis.

Overall, +1. A solid SCP on its own. A good springboard when viewed in light of the recent serial.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Calling it now, the new SCP-001 is Yivo! Think about it, it's a universe-sized space monster that sees Earth as a single living entity (thanks to SCP-2798, apparently). Mystery solved.

On a more serious note, I really enjoyed this one. The mere idea of what SCP-2798 is, and that we made it, is just horrifying, and the rest of the article, especially the Suppressed Documentation Log, does a great job of amping up that sense of dread. Here I was hoping the souls weren't "alive" or conscious, or that at least they weren't aware of what had been done to them. No such luck.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I discovered this only yesterday, and so didn't have the anticipation necessary to get aboard the hype dirigible. I dig it, and as someone who has studied civil society and its effects on… normal society, I guess, the bit about lack of participation in mass movements actually creeped me out more than a little bit. The idea itself was quite interesting, and it's always fun to see the Foundation trying to deal with a slow-motion train crash.

The only complaint that I have is that while it was effective, I feel like you leaned on the word salad horror stuff a bit much. A little bit goes a long way, in my opinion. Still, quite awesome. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: but how do we force 001 to take it

item.title: Re: Stay Tuned
item.content:encoded: The hype train has derailed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I heard that every morning, Kalinin eats a newbie for breakfast.

Without any milk.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this feels like an honorary 001 anyway

item.title: Stay Tuned
item.content:encoded: Technical difficulties related to only being on a phone everyone. Things should be going in a few hours.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: boo where's my free cyanide pill

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 1 hour to go. All is lost.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The end is nigh!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
> A v > A v

I was just alerted to the existence of this article, and it is twelve minutes until the end. The hype is real.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I heard Kalinin was the real author of Shakespeare's plays.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not for me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's it. The timer has reached zero. Now we wait.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ever see Kalinin without his mask on? I hear his face is the best.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought I'd just let yall know that the people over at the subreddit have been hyping this shit up1 and that they will be looking forward to the new 001 proposal.

Footnotes
1. Which is the reason for recent amount of upvotes.

item.title: 3 HOURS TO GO
item.content:encoded: IT'S HAPPENING.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Found Kalinin's alt account.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I heard that Kalinin is a mass delusion and we are leaving comments on a blank page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've heard that Kalinin has an eight-pack, that he's shredded.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I heard that once the timer reaches zero, the author is going to release their concept for SCP-001.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very interested to see what happens in two days! This was the first Thaumiel I read that left me with a sense of doom that usually accompanies some of the nastier Keter articles.

item.title: 2 days
item.content:encoded: Brace yrself, its coming to an end

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So close, and yet so far. It's a shame I never look at Recent Changes….I might've caught the little revisions to the third addenda Kal's been doing as they happened.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: THREE DAYS

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, but the supposed follow up will determine how interesting it can get. The rather jarring appearance of the timer caught me off guard and serves as a good text jump scare, but….then I thought about it. Unless a follow up is planned for that day, it's just so much [redacted] = scary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Another skip where the Foundation administration is so incompetent that they basically arrange for a counter-intuitive outcome where everyone dies anyway. Woah, oh, so scary!!!

And "Transcendent Identity Disorder?" Apparently, it has no treatment…except it's treated from psychiatric institutions just like any other multi-personality disorder. It's so hard to take seriously. How is that even cohesively realistic? There's a threshold to mass-SCP cheese where it can only get so bad, and I think that someone "going crazy" because of the singularity just isn't even supported enough by mainstream science to make it remotely plausible.

Also, the chat logs? Unbelievable. That took me back 7 years ago when I used to read haunted video game creepypastas.
